cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==). This assigns 5 to x and always evaluates to true.
cards.explanation: The single = is assignment, not comparison. The expression (x = 5) assigns 5 to x, then evaluates to 5 (non-zero, which is true). Use == for comparison.

cards.text:
For equality comparison, use {{c1::==}} (equality operator).
For assignment, use {{c2::=}} (assignment operator).

cards.explanation:
To prevent accidentally using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==) in if conditions, many developers use these techniques:
  • Turn on compiler warnings (most compilers warn about this)
  • Use constants on the left side: if (5 == x) instead of if (x == 5) - this causes a compile error if you use =
  • Modern C++ compilers with -Wall or -Wextra will warn about this
examples: if (5 == x)  // Good: constant on left

cards.question: What will this code print?
cards.explanation: Without braces, only the first statement (std::cout << "a") is part of the if. The second statement (std::cout << "b") always executes. Indentation is misleading - always use braces!

cards.front: What is the dangling else problem?
cards.back: When nested if statements don't use braces, it's ambiguous which if an else belongs to. The else attaches to the nearest if, which may not be what the programmer intended.

cards.question: Which if does the else belong to in this code?
cards.answer: The else belongs to the inner if (y > 0), not the outer if (x > 0).
cards.explanation: An else clause is attached to the nearest unmatched if in the same block. Despite the misleading indentation, the else pairs with if (y > 0). Always use braces to avoid this ambiguity!

cards.text: To avoid the dangling else problem, always use {{c1::braces}} (curly brackets) with nested if statements.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Semicolon after the if condition creates a null statement. The cout always executes regardless of the condition.
cards.explanation: The semicolon terminates the if with an empty statement. The cout is not part of the if. This is a very common and subtle bug.

cards.front: Why is it recommended to avoid using == and != with floating point values?
cards.back: Floating point values can have small rounding errors, so two values that should be equal may not compare as exactly equal. Use an epsilon comparison instead.

cards.text: To chain multiple conditions where only one should be true, use {{c1::if}} for the first, {{c2::else if}} for middle conditions, and {{c3::else}} for the default case.

cards.front: In an if-else if-else chain, what happens when one condition is true?
cards.back: When a condition is true, its associated statement executes and the rest of the chain is skipped. Only one block in the chain will execute.

cards.explanation:
Follow these rules to avoid common if statement bugs:
  • Always use braces { } even for single statements
  • Use == for comparison, not = (assignment)
  • Watch for semicolons after if conditions (creates null statement)
  • Be aware of the dangling else problem in nested ifs
  • Don't compare floating point values with == or !=
  • Use proper indentation (but don't trust it without braces)
